artifact_refs.name: enveda/ccs-prediction
artifact_refs.prose: Raw SMILES strings from CCS dataset (enveda/ccs-prediction repository)

data_in.description: Raw SMILES strings from CCS dataset (enveda/ccs-prediction repository)

data_out.description: Serialized graph dataset file containing atom/bond feature tensors and molecular graphs in PyTorch or pickle format

domain_knowledge: SMILES strings represent molecular structures as linear notation; canonicalization ensures consistent representation of the same molecule across multiple input formats.

Atom features typically include atomic number, formal charge, hybridization state, and aromaticity; bond features include bond type (single/double/triple/aromatic), stereochemistry, and conjugation status.
RDKit is the standard open-source library for SMILES parsing, molecular graph construction, and feature extraction in cheminformatics.
Graph neural networks for molecular property prediction consume node feature matrices (atoms) and edge feature matrices (bonds) as input, requiring proper tensor serialization.
Feature tensor formats must match the downstream GNN architecture's expected input shape and normalization conventions.

direct_checks: verify that the repository github:enveda/ccs-prediction contains a preprocessing script or module that accepts SMILES strings as input
verify that the preprocessing pipeline produces a serialized graph dataset file in a standard format (.pt, .pkl, or equivalent)
verify that the output graph objects contain node features (atom features) and edge features (bond features) as tensors or array-like structures
script_runs: execute the preprocessing pipeline on a subset of the CCS dataset and confirm it completes without errors
verify that the generated graph dataset file is not empty and contains at least one graph object
verify that graph objects are compatible with a standard GNN framework (PyTorch Geometric, DGL, or equivalent) by attempting to load and inspect one example graph
expert_review: assess whether the atom/bond feature engineering choices (feature dimensionality, encoding scheme, normalization) are appropriate for collision cross section prediction
evaluate whether the graph construction methodology (bond connectivity rules, handling of implicit hydrogens, stereochemistry encoding) correctly represents the molecular structure
review whether the dataset preprocessing preserves the chemical validity and integrity of the original SMILES representations

cmd: import sys; from rdkit import Chem; import torch; import pickle; smiles_data = open('{inputs.raw_smiles}').read().strip().split('\n'); graphs = []; [graphs.append({'smiles': smi, 'mol': Chem.MolFromSmiles(smi), 'atoms': [atom.GetAtomicNum() for atom in Chem.MolFromSmiles(smi).GetAtoms()] if Chem.MolFromSmiles(smi) else []}) for smi in smiles_data if smi]; torch.save({'graphs': graphs, 'metadata': {'count': len(graphs), 'source': 'ccs_dataset'}}, 'molecular_graphs_{task_id}.pt'); print(f'Serialized {len(graphs)} molecular graphs to molecular_graphs_{task_id}.pt')

inputs: Raw SMILES strings from CCS dataset (enveda/ccs-prediction repository)
methodology_summary: Load raw SMILES strings from the CCS dataset repository.
Canonicalize each SMILES string using RDKit to ensure structural uniqueness.
Convert each canonical SMILES to a molecular graph representation.
Extract atom-level features (atomic number, charge, hybridization, aromaticity) and bond-level features (type, aromaticity, stereochemistry).
Construct node and edge feature tensors encoding molecular structure.
Serialize all graph objects and associated metadata to a standard PyTorch or pickle format.
Validation: confirm output file exists and contains graph tensors with shapes consistent with expected atom/bond dimensions.
missing_information: No changelog provided documenting modifications or versions of the preprocessing code

workflow_description: 1. Load raw SMILES strings from the CCS dataset repository (enveda/ccs-prediction). 2. Canonicalize SMILES using RDKit to ensure structural consistency. 3. Convert each canonical SMILES to a molecular graph representation with atom and bond features. 4. Extract atom-level features (atomic number, formal charge, hybridization, aromaticity) and bond-level features (bond type, aromaticity, stereochemistry). 5. Construct feature tensors encoding atom and bond adjacency matrices. 6. Serialize all graph objects and metadata to a standard PyTorch (.pt) or pickle (.pkl) format.
